body: Should feel like Apple Notes — show up and use, no thinking required. Super intuitive.
Airy, light, easy to use. Must not feel like a tech product at all.
Light mode, not dark. Less masculine energy. Remove visible borders/lines between sections — use whitespace and hover-reveal instead.
Reduce clutter aggressively. Current space-page-chat feels overly cluttered and too techie — too much visible chrome and structure.
Assistants are user-scoped, not tied to a space or area. User invites them into any context, like Discord permissions. Must be first-class in every direction.
Core hierarchy: Workspace > Area (Home) > Space (Place) > Items. Chat exists at Area level (dump) and Space level (focused). Items replace Views with flexible kind labels.
Area is the hard boundary for AI context retrieval. Dump chat in Area becomes ambient context for all child Spaces. Cross-Area retrieval does not happen.
Item kind is a flexible string label (doc, spec, note, tracker, dashboard, journal, checklist, etc). Shell must be kind-agnostic — same open/pin/search/link behavior for all kinds.
User-facing language: Area = Home, Space = Place (with # prefix), Items = just the name + kind badge. Avoid system nouns in the UI.
Current item browser (pinned/recent/folders) feels like a bad approach. Item organization/discovery is an open design problem — needs multiple explorations.
Too cluttered, too techie. Too much going on — hard to figure out. Item browser organization doesn't work. Needs radical simplification.
Light four-zone: search-first. Same layout bones but light mode, airy. Browser panel is just a search box + results. No folders, no pinned/recent sections. Philosophy: you don't browse, you recall.
Light three-zone: inline items. Kill the browser panel entirely. Items appear as collapsible section within nav sidebar under each Place. Three zones instead of four. Simpler but does it scale?

Light four-zone: popover browser. Browser is not a permanent panel — it's a popover/dropdown summoned by clicking near the Place name. Like Notion page links or Spotlight. Four zones when needed, two when not.
Cards + tabs. Areas as top-level tabs. Content organized as scannable cards — places, items, chat previews. Spatial and visual. Easy to scan.
Desktop tiling. Draggable, resizable, pinnable windows. More like an OS workspace. User arranges their own layout. Chat, items, browser as separate tileable panes.

Spatial canvas. Infinite zoomable surface like Figma/Miro. Areas as regions of the canvas. Items as objects. Chat happens in-context near the things it's about. Zoom in to focus, out to see landscape.
Timeline journal. Chronological spine as the main organizing principle. Areas as color-coded bands. Items as waypoints. Scroll through time to find things. For people who think temporally.

Conversation + drawers. Chat fills entire screen. Navigation, items, browser slide in as temporary drawers/overlays from edges. Philosophy: conversation is primary, everything else is secondary and on-demand.
Single-column feed. No sidebar at all. One scrolling surface. Switch areas with minimal top picker. Items and chat interleaved. Apple Notes energy. Extremely simple, extremely opinionated.
Terminal/command-driven direction — too techie, violates the non-technical feel constraint.
Kanban/columns direction — feels too much like a project management tool, not a conversation-first product.
Split-screen pairs — too constrained, doesn't explore enough of the design space.

All three use cases (Chef, Pebble, Coaching) share the same workflow shape: start messy → think over time → preserve what matters → revisit without starting over → turn vague thought into structured action. The difference is the domain and what success looks like.

Chef case is the full creative cycle: idea capture → menu ideation → dish development → logistics → post-dinner review. Core pain is fragmentation — creative work can't compound because ideas, recipes, costs, and lessons are scattered. Success = taste compounds over time.

Pebble case is co-founder collaboration: broad ideation → focused product work → founder syncs → decision capture → launch planning. Core pain is conversation decay — ideas discussed then forgotten, decisions scattered. Success = shared understanding compounds.

Coaching case combines life accountability, fitness/training, and reflection/therapy. Core need is continuity — not generic advice but accountability over time, pattern recognition, honest challenge. Success = self-understanding compounds.

Five cross-case requirements every direction must support: (1) start with unfinished thought, (2) focus without losing broader context, (3) durable outputs that matter later, (4) easy re-entry, (5) compounding value over time.

Shared seed data module created at .design/studio/app/lib/seed-data.ts. All prototypes import from here so directions are directly comparable. Covers 3 homes, 9 places, ~15 items, 8 assistants, chat with @mentions, feed entries.

Best power-user layout (5/5 daily driver). Only prototype where you can see nav, items, chat, AND detail simultaneously without losing context. But dark mode is masculine/techie and fights the brand goal. Structure is right, visual treatment is wrong.

Best balance of simplicity + capability (4/5 power user, 4/5 design). Popover browser with backdrop blur feels polished and intentional. Clean by default, powerful when summoned. Warm palette. Closest to Apple Notes of the four-zone variants. The Browse button could be more discoverable.

Most beautiful prototype (4.5/5 design). Card layout with kind badges is visually rich without clutter. Assistant pills with Invite button are elegant. Great for scanning/overview. But drilling into a Place loses the overview — needs a better focused-work mode beneath the card grid.

Most flexible layout (4/5 power user). User arranges their own workspace. Chat + item side by side is the key workflow and this nails it. Warm OS energy. Needs snap-to-grid badly — without it, windows feel chaotic. With snap: could be 5/5.

Clean and distinctive (4/5 design). Vertical timeline with colored area dots is elegant. VOICES contributor row is a nice touch. Good scanning surface. But it's a reading/review view, not a working view — no place-level navigation or focused chat. Strong as a secondary view, weak as primary workspace.

Warmest palette of all prototypes (#FEFCF9 cream/linen). Feels most like Apple Notes emotionally. But too minimal when drawers are closed — not enough on screen to work with. Drawer transitions slow down workflows. Can't reference item AND chat simultaneously.

Simplest of all (1/5 cognitive load). iMessage for work. But too constrained — can't see chat and item at same time. Everything inline means constant scrolling. Beautiful rhythm but can't support the full product workflow.

Most confusing prototype (5/5 cognitive load). Too many things on screen with no clear reading order. Fun for brainstorming, terrible for daily use. At scale (50+ items) becomes overwhelming. Miro energy, not Apple Notes energy.
Search-only browser is a problem — you can't browse, only recall. Discovery of forgotten items is gone. The empty center area isn't calm, it's incomplete. Nice warm palette but feels unfinished.

Items nested under Places in sidebar is intuitive hierarchically. But sidebar gets very long at scale (10 places × 5 items = 50+ entries). Warm stone palette. Emoji home switcher pills are nice. Tilde/hash prefixes feel a bit code-y.

Core design tension: simple ≠ capable. The simplest UIs (single-column, chat-layout) score 1/5 for power use. The most capable (space-page-chat) scores 4/5 for cognitive load. The popover browser pattern and desktop tiling are the two best approaches for resolving this tension.

The warm cream palette from conversation-drawers (#FEFCF9) should be the base for all future iterations. It's the warmest, least techie color treatment. Combined with the stone/amber accents from light-inline-items.

Best item treatments: (1) Kind badges from cards-tabs (color-coded pills) are the best type indicator. (2) Popover browser (summon-on-demand) is the best discovery pattern. (3) Inline-under-place in sidebar works for small counts but doesn't scale.

Four directions worth pursuing in round 2: (1) Space-page-chat structure + popover-browser visual treatment (the synthesis). (2) Cards-tabs as overview with better drill-down. (3) Desktop tiling with snap-to-grid. (4) Timeline as a secondary/companion view.
Spatial canvas direction killed — too confusing, wrong energy, doesn't scale. Miro, not Apple Notes.
Single-column feed killed as primary layout — too constrained for the product. Can't support simultaneous chat + item reference. Could survive as a mobile/narrow view.
Light search-first killed — search-only discovery is a dead end. Users need to browse, not just recall.

Still the strongest direction. Best bones. But missing the ability to tab/organize information — limits how much you can see at once and forces too much switching. Needs better information organization within the layout.

Interesting iteration on space-page-chat. Layout is potentially good but not obvious to use. Screenshot was cut off — missing the search bar, which is important to the design. Rendering issues need fixing in the gallery.
Interesting but just not right. Something feels off. Not pursuing further.

The TABS for areas are interesting — inherently organizable. But the card grid itself isn't useful. The only thing worth taking forward is using tabs for area switching. Chat should be first and foremost, not cards.
New user issues are real, but if we snap to grid by default (everyone's used a computer), it becomes obvious. Usability needs work. Worth taking further with snap-to-grid as default behavior.
Interesting but too much. Also has rendering/cutoff issues in the gallery — top of the design is clipped. Confirms kill.

The timeline with days split up showing what's been happening is really cool as an AREA FEED view. When you jump into an area, you see a timeline of recent activity with chat at the bottom. Not a standalone layout — a way to render the home/area feed.
Just doesn't feel right. Warm palette is interesting and potentially friendliest, but clean light mode might be fine too. Not pursuing as a direction.
Interesting as a way to think about an area feed — similar to timeline journal. Both single-column and timeline do the area feed well. Not a standalone layout, but a feed rendering pattern.

Tabs for area switching (from cards-tabs) are worth incorporating into other layouts. They're inherently organizable and intuitive. The card grid below them is not useful — chat should be primary, not cards.

Both timeline-journal and single-column-feed demonstrate good area feed patterns. The timeline with day separators and recent activity overview is especially compelling as the view you see when entering an Area/Home. Chat input at the bottom to dump into the area.

Gallery rendering issue: prototypes are getting cut off depending on how they scale. Screenshots miss important UI elements (search bars, top sections). Need fixed-aspect-ratio 'screen desktop' containers that scale prototypes consistently.
Light inline items direction killed — doesn't feel right. Not pursuing further.
Conversation + drawers direction killed — doesn't feel right. Warm palette noted but direction dropped.
Round 2 directions: (1) Space-page-chat evolved with tabs + warm light treatment. (2) Desktop tiling with snap-to-grid default. (3) Timeline/feed as the area-level view pattern within either layout.

Round 2 generated: 6 prototypes. 4 full layouts (tabs-popover-light, tabs-collapsible, tabs-split-pane, tiling-snap-grid) + 2 component showcases (feed-explorations with 4 feed variants, browser-explorations with 4 browser variants). All use shared seed data, w-full h-full sizing, warm light palette.

Fixed gallery rendering: all prototypes now use w-full h-full instead of viewport units. Layout wrapper injects CSS variables (--container-width, --container-height). Prototype viewer has device preset toggles (Desktop/Laptop/Tablet/Mobile). Sections render newest-first.
Don't use 'Home' terminology for areas. Areas are the top-level concept — clusters or groups in your life. They sit at the very top level alongside the logo/brand.

Areas should feel like workspace switching (one active at a time), not browser tabs. Each area is a different context/cluster. Switching changes everything below. Clean separation. Account level includes all areas you're part of.

Tiling is the direction. Layouts should be user-definable, not preset. Per-area arrangement — each area remembers how you last arranged windows. Possibly save named layouts too, but the system should auto-remember as baseline.

Assistants need deeper thinking. They follow the user around. User can invite them to specific places/projects. Need an assistant library/database concept. Must be extremely simple to bring one in. They're separate from navigation — not sidebar items.

Memory/knowledge is hierarchical: account level includes all areas. Within an area, all content is available (that's the retrieval boundary). Spaces/places scope what the system has access to at that point. This hierarchy matters for both UX and AI context.

None of the Round 4 prototypes feel quite right, but each has useful elements. Tabbed items panel is useful. Persistent place tabs are right for context preservation. Tiling feels right for flexibility. Pinned sidebar items are clever. No single prototype combines them correctly yet.
Bottom dock/bar as window launcher is interesting but not confirmed. The concept of having something at the bottom is appealing but the right content isn't clear yet. Needs more exploration.

Assistant access is USER-scoped, not shared. If two users both have access to the same assistant, each user's context is isolated. The assistant only retrieves from the scope of the conversation it's in FOR THAT USER. An assistant in my personal space can't leak to another user who also uses that assistant.

Assistant scope is both memory boundary AND visibility. Area-scoped = shows up everywhere in the area, has access to everything in the area (like an area-scoped human). Space-scoped = only shows up in that space, only accesses things in that space. Scope-level access depends on where you're communicating from.

An assistant with access to multiple areas (e.g., a private chat) should be able to reference shared context — knowing what spaces you're both in, what it has access to — while avoiding accidentally retrieving wrong context. The retrieval must be intersection-aware.

Multiple entry points for bringing assistants in: (1) @mention auto-invite — first mention asks 'Should I add X to this space?' (2) Drag and drop from roster (3) Share/permissions dialog. None should be everywhere by default — access is deliberate, not ambient.

Multi-agent interaction has three tiers: (1) Now: assistants only respond to user. (2) Next: user can explicitly ask them to collaborate ('work this out together'). (3) Future: assistants can respond to each other and act proactively. Design the UI to support tier 1 now, but don't close the door on 2 and 3.

Product ships with basic default assistants (e.g., 'Peb' — always available everywhere, the default). Users create their own through a bot creation flow that's abstracted and simple. This is a big part of the product value.
There should be a default assistant ('Peb' or similar) that's always available everywhere regardless of scope. It's the baseline — you can always talk to it.
Layout is layered: (1) top bar with logo + area selector, (2) space tabs below, (3) workspace with pinned chat + snappable panes, (4) bottom bar. This is the target structure.
Per-SPACE layouts, not per-area. Each space remembers its own pane arrangement. #Menus has menu+budget side by side, #Recipes has a different arrangement.
Chat is always pinned on the left side. It never disappears. The right side is free-form snappable panes for items, browser, feed, etc.
Area selector lives in the top bar alongside the logo. It IS the primary navigation. One active at a time. Switching changes everything below.
Drop 'Home' terminology entirely. Areas are just areas — Pebble, Chef, Coaching. No special naming.

Private/DM spaces exist outside the area hierarchy. Retrieval in a private chat = union of what you and the other participant share. If an assistant has Chef + Coaching access, a DM with it can pull from both. Exception to the area-as-hard-wall rule.

Re-themed unified-refined from generic workspace tool to coaching beachhead for founders. Areas become coaching domains (Career/Relationships/Wellness), assistants become named coaches with personalities, added memory panel with trust indicators. Aligns prototype with investor memo positioning.

Tolan research: embodiment transforms AI from tool to presence. Key moves: (1) animated shape-characters with faces — eyebrows are critical for expression despite looking goofy, (2) state machine of keyframe + procedural animations (IK for natural movement, keyframes for emotional beats), (3) parallel emotional tone adaptation system monitors conversation and adjusts character delivery independently from LLM, (4) deliberately non-human shapes avoid uncanny valley while remaining deeply expressive, (5) voice-first centered-character layout makes it feel like talking TO someone not typing AT something. Designed by Eran Hilleli (Chantlings) and animated by Chromosphere. Raised $20M.

Coach character system direction: each Pebble coach becomes a geometric shape-character with face (dot eyes, line eyebrows, curved mouth) and signature accessory. Shapes inherit brand language but evolve it — not just circles/triangles but distinct forms per coach personality. Key new surface: a 'Talk to [Coach]' tile showing the character large and centered with expression states, simplified chat below, warm gradient in coach color. Expression states respond to conversation mood (concern, warmth, challenge, curiosity). This is the emotional layer that makes coaching feel like a relationship, not a search query.

The shape-character coach idea bridges the gap between Pebble's brand design language (geometric shapes) and Tolan's insight that embodiment creates trust. Pebble already uses circles for people and triangles for agents — evolving these into expressive characters with faces is a natural extension, not a new system. The 'Talk to' tile is the single strongest differentiator from ChatGPT/Claude: you're not messaging an API, you're sitting with someone who sees you.

Round 13 feedback: Living Journal is 'really cool desktop entry point — what we hoped the feed would be.' Quiet Hours vibe is right but needs to lead somewhere. Two Surfaces concept works, faces are cute, go-deeper is cool. Coach-Specific UX is interesting for thinking about previous designs. Memory Home interesting but wrong as main entry. Group Session feels like just a chat — not special enough. One Thread bad except it shows multi-agent conversation naturally. Responsive Workspace is actually a good workspace — eventually needed. The Ritual doesn't give enough to understand how it works. Voice should always be available alongside text.

BREAKTHROUGH: The conversation IS the product surface. Everything else is pulled out of it. Coaches create artifacts inline (charts, trackers, frameworks, logs) that render as rich cards in the conversation. When you need more space, artifacts expand into side panels — that's where the tiling system from Rounds 7-11 earns its existence. Not as a workspace you navigate to, but as expansion of conversation content. No separate items section. No 'go to workspace' moment. Artifacts exist because a coach made them. They live in conversation history. The workspace IS the conversation plus whatever you've expanded alongside it. Mobile: things stay inline, tap to expand full-screen. Desktop: side-by-side tiling because you have the space. Coaches ARE the navigation — you don't browse areas/topics, you talk to people.

Coaches should create tools for you — fitness log, weight tracker, decision frameworks. These aren't separate app features; they're things a coach builds during a conversation that persist and update over time. The 'personal software' layer from the memo emerges naturally from coaching conversations, not from a workspace UI.

Multi-agent conversation is critical. Coaches should naturally chime in on each other's conversations. Maya helping with fundraise prep while Luna notices sleep affecting pitch confidence — this cross-domain moment needs to happen in one conversation, not require switching spaces. This is the thing that's impossible with human coaches and impossible with ChatGPT.

Voice and text must always both be available. Voice is a core modality for coaching (you tell a therapist things aloud you'd never type), but founders in open offices can't always talk. Both inputs should always be present, not a mode toggle.

DESIGN JOURNEY NARRATIVE — How we arrived at conversation-first with inline artifacts:

**Starting point:** 12 rounds of workspace design (sidebar, areas, spaces, items, tiling). Two finalists: arc-tiles v5 and conversation-artifact v3. Then unified-refined polished these into a cohesive desktop workspace.

**The memo changed everything:** Chris shared the investor memo positioning Pebble as 'AI coaches that remember you.' We realized the prototypes and the memo were telling two different stories. The workspace led with structure; the memo led with trust and relationships.

**Re-theme (v4-v5):** We re-themed unified-refined with coaching content, named coaches (Maya, James, Aria, Kai, Luna, Rex), memory panel, trust indicators. Then added shape-character SVGs with faces and expressions inspired by Tolan research. Created the Talk-to tile.

**The sunk cost tension:** Chris named the core tension: 'What I was building through most of the iterations was trying to be a workspace, when coaching is the initial thing. The workspace does all the things our future thing wants apart from the explicit coaching.' This felt like sunk cost — 12 rounds of workspace design vs. a coaching-first entry.

**Resolution attempt — progressive reveal:** I proposed three layers: check-in (door) → coach circle (room) → workspace (desk). Chris pushed back: 'How do we design for coaching but expand to what we were initially designing for?'

**Key insight — agents with memory:** The universal primitive isn't coaching, it's the agent-with-memory. Maya the coach and Chef the culinary collaborator are architecturally identical. Coaching is how you earn trust to become personal software.

**Round 13 — 12 argument prototypes:** Built prototypes to kill bad instincts and test good directions. Chris's feedback:
- Living Journal: 'Really cool entry point for the desktop — that's what we hoped the feed would be'
- Quiet Hours: 'I like the vibe'
- Two Surfaces: 'Interesting concept, faces are cute, go-deeper is cool'
- One Thread: bad direction BUT 'the one nice thing is it shows talking to multiple agents at once'
- Memory Home: 'Interesting but not the main entry'
- Group Session: 'Feels like just a chat, not special'
- The Ritual: 'Doesn't give me much to work on'
- Coaches should create tools: 'If I have a fitness coach a fitness log just makes sense. Simple things like that.'

**Chris named the REAL tension:** 'The connection from these designs to the workspace concept feels disjoint. I'm trying to figure out how we start at coaching and eventually allow all the functionality we captured in round 11. We want to capture the functionality but I don't care about matching the layout.'

**The breakthrough:** Chris: 'Maybe the artifacts aren't as important if you can render graphics and things inline in the chat and have them expand out in the side when you need them. It's like conversation first but then tiling and all these other things on the side are possible. You can play with those as necessary as you talk.'

**Where we landed:** The conversation IS the product. Coaches create tools inline. Artifacts render as rich cards in conversation. Need more space? They expand into side panels (the tiling system). No separate workspace mode. No items section. Coaches ARE the navigation. Mobile: inline cards, tap to expand. Desktop: conversation + side-by-side expansion. The workspace from Rounds 1-12 earns its existence not as a destination but as an expansion of conversation content.

PERSONA RESEARCH SYNTHESIS — 6 founder personas evaluated 10 wireframes. Results:

CLEAR WINNER: #8 Conversation + Drawer — chosen by 5 of 6 as what they'd actually use (not what they'd say). The full-screen conversation with hidden drawer was described as: 'the closed door' (Elena), 'nowhere to hide' (Sarah), 'respects my attention' (Kenji), 'the only one that would get me to say the real thing' (Sarah), 'like David's office with the door shut at 11pm' (Elena).

STRONG SECOND: #9 Two-Column Journal — chosen by 3 personas for the longitudinal value. Elena: 'I'd scroll the left column and see all the Sunday nights I was afraid, and realize every time Monday came I was fine.' Sarah: 'it made me feel like there's a future version of me on the other side of Thursday.' Priya: only if the journal auto-generates from conversations, not if she has to write it.

SURPRISE: #4 Notebook — David (least technical, most emotional chaos) picked this because 'the containers loosened something in my chest.' The categories gave structure to his chaos even though he'd never say he wants structure. Marcus was haunted by it — the empty Relationships folder showed him his life's imbalance.

CONSISTENTLY REJECTED: #3 Coach-First Nav (too intimate too early, 'feels like choosing a therapist'), #6 Browser Tabs (feels like work, 'another set of tabs I feel guilty about'), #10 Lobby (most screenshot-worthy, least used — 'design award energy, 3% retention').

KEY INSIGHT: The gap between 'official pick' and 'real pick' was consistent. Power users (Marcus, Elena, Priya) officially chose Command Bar or Tabs but admitted Drawer or Journal would be what they'd open at night. The stated preference is efficiency; the actual need is immersion and being seen.

DAVID'S OBSERVATION (most important): 'The gap between what people SAY they want (simple, human, just texting) versus what actually helps them (structure, containers, a place for each part of the chaos). I need someone to organize me right now. I just don't want it to FEEL like organizing.'

Conversation Core is the synthesis of Rounds 1-14. Drawer won 5/6 persona tests. Journal won as longitudinal view. Coaches create structure organically. Artifacts expand from conversation. This is the direction.

CONVERSATION SESSION SUMMARY (Mar 21-23, 2026) — Comprehensive design session spanning 3 days, starting from investor memo analysis through to polished production prototype.

PHASE 1: MEMO ANALYSIS → RE-THEME (Rounds 11 v4-v5)
Started by reading the investor memo positioning Pebble as 'AI coaches that remember you.' Identified gap between workspace-first prototypes and coaching-first memo. Re-themed unified-refined with coaching content, named coaches (Maya/James/Aria/Kai/Luna/Rex), memory panel, trust indicators. Added shape-character SVG system with faces and expressions inspired by Tolan research.

PHASE 2: TENSION & EXPLORATION (Rounds 13-14)
Chris named the core tension: 'What I was building was trying to be a workspace, when coaching is the initial thing.' Built 12 argument prototypes (Round 13) to kill bad instincts and test directions. Ran 6 founder persona evaluations — Conversation+Drawer won 5/6. Built 10 wireframe directions (Round 14) exploring navigation models.

PHASE 3: BREAKTHROUGH — CONVERSATION FIRST
Chris: 'Maybe the artifacts aren't as important if you can render graphics and things inline in the chat and have them expand out in the side when you need them.' The conversation IS the product. Coaches create tools inline. Artifacts expand into side panels. No separate workspace mode.

PHASE 4: POLISHED PROTOTYPE (conversation-polished)
Rebuilt from scratch as ~50 clean files. CVA primitives, Tailwind tokens, proportional coach faces. Code reviewed and tightened. All 19 screen states functional.

PHASE 5: UX REFINEMENTS (via Agentation feedback)
- 'Conversations' renamed to 'Spaces' — long-term, not disposable
- Coach roster with DM access — click coach to DM, 3-dot for profile
- DMs implicit to coaches, not shown in Spaces list
- Sidebar full-height — pushes top bar to right column
- Quick replies merged into composer
- Inline choice dialogs (radio + multi-select) added
- Conversation name+faces = single button opening conversation info
- Coach profiles are drill-down from conversation info

PERSONA RESEARCH METHOD — Ran 6 founder personas (Sarah/Marcus/Priya/David/Kenji/Elena) against 10 wireframes. Each persona had: a specific emotional scenario (not neutral evaluation), built-in contradictions (stated vs actual preferences), and narrated behavior (stream of consciousness, not opinions). Key finding: people say they want efficiency but actually need immersion. Conversation+Drawer won because it strips away excuses to organize instead of feel. David's quote: 'The gap between what people SAY they want (simple, just texting) versus what actually helps them (structure, containers). I need someone to organize me right now. I just don't want it to FEEL like organizing.'

SPACES VS CONVERSATIONS — Renamed 'Conversations' to 'Spaces' throughout the UI. Rationale from Chris: 'Conversations imply they are like normal AI conversations where our conversations are long term.' Spaces are persistent, topic-scoped, can have multiple coaches. DMs are implicit 1:1 connections tied to coach roster, not shown in the Spaces list. This maps to: Spaces ≈ Slack channels, DMs ≈ Slack direct messages, Coaches ≈ contacts list.

SIDEBAR LAYOUT — Full-height sidebar that pushes top bar to the right column. Layout: sidebar | (top bar + content). Matches Slack/Discord pattern where the sidebar is the leftmost column spanning full height. Sidebar has expand/collapse toggle — expanded shows full labels, collapsed shows icon rail (~60px). The hamburger menu moved into the sidebar header, inline with Spaces/Journal/Your Stuff tabs.

CONVERSATION INFO AS PRIMARY ACTION — Clicking the conversation name + coach faces in the top bar opens conversation info (not individual coach profiles). Rationale from Chris: 'Memory will largely be controlled at the space level so this is important.' Coach profiles are a secondary drill-down accessed from within the conversation info panel. This makes space-level memory management the primary action from the header.

COACH DM MODEL — Clicking a coach in the sidebar roster opens a persistent DM (finds existing, creates only if none exists). DMs don't appear in the Spaces list — they're implicit to the coach relationship. Coach rows show name + role (people directory style), not last message previews. 3-dot menu on hover for: View profile, Edit coach, Remove coach. This keeps coaches feeling like people you talk to, not an inbox you manage.

INLINE CHOICE DIALOGS — Coaches can present structured choices inline in conversation: radio (single-select) for decisions like 'How do you want to open your pitch?', multi-select (checkboxes) for assessments like 'Which of these disrupt your sleep?' Options highlight on selection, confirm button appears, response is recorded. Quick reply suggestions moved into the composer as floating pills above the text input — arrow-key navigable, same visual unit as the input.

TOLAN RESEARCH TAKEAWAYS — Tolan ($20M raised) validates embodiment transforms AI from tool to presence. Key moves: (1) simple animated shapes with faces — eyebrows critical for expression, (2) state machine of keyframe + procedural animations, (3) parallel emotional tone adaptation independent from LLM, (4) non-human shapes avoid uncanny valley, (5) voice-first centered character feels like talking TO someone. Our coach face system (diamond/squircle/circle/hexagon/blob with dot eyes, mouth, eyebrows) follows this pattern. Scaling fixed with viewBox='0 0 100 100' normalization.

DESIGN STUDIO INFRASTRUCTURE FIXES — Several issues discovered and fixed in the gallery infrastructure during this session:
1. Prototype layout had bg-white overriding prototype backgrounds → removed, prototypes control their own bg
2. Tailwind v4 @utility directives in prototype-local CSS files aren't processed → moved Pebble tokens to globals.css @theme block
3. Gallery dark mode toggle cascades into prototypes → prototype layout now removes .dark from html on mount, restores on unmount
4. Agentation component needed endpoint='http://localhost:4747' prop to connect to MCP server
5. Prototype container overflow-auto → changed to overflow-hidden to keep composer pinned

THE SUNK COST RESOLUTION — 12 rounds of workspace design (sidebar, areas, spaces, items, tiling) felt like sunk cost when coaching became the entry point. The resolution: the workspace isn't the wrong product, it's the wrong LAYER to lead with. The tiling mechanics, artifact viewers, snap dividers all survive — they show up as expansion of conversation content, not as a destination. Chris: 'We want to capture the functionality but I don't care about matching the layout.' The workspace from Rounds 1-12 earns its existence not as a shell you navigate, but as what expands out of a conversation when you need room.

LIVING JOURNAL AS DESKTOP ENTRY — Chris's strongest reaction in Round 13 was to the Living Journal prototype: 'Really cool entry point for the desktop — that's what we hoped the feed would be.' The feed/journal concept was always the intent behind the home feed in earlier rounds, but the workspace framing buried it under navigation structure. The journal timeline became the default landing in conversation-polished — coach faces at top, greeting, composer card, proactive nudge from Maya, then scrollable timeline of auto-generated entries (patterns, milestones, commitments, insights, reflections). The journal builds itself from conversations. The user discovers their growth by scrolling, not by writing.

INITIAL GAP IDENTIFIED — The investor memo positions Pebble as 'AI coaches that remember you.' The unified-refined prototype told a different story: a workspace tool with sidebar, areas, spaces, items. Five specific gaps: (1) seed data showed generic workspace (Chef/Pebble/Coaching) not founder coaching, (2) UI led with structure not trust, (3) assistants were generic triangles not coaches with personality, (4) memory was invisible — zero UI for what Pebble knows about you, (5) multi-coach architecture wasn't visible. The prototype and the memo were speaking two different languages.

CHRIS: 'These coaches should be able to create tools for you to help you achieve your things. If I have a fitness coach a fitness log just makes sense or a weight tracker makes sense.' This was the moment coaches went from conversationalists to tool-creators. Coaches don't just talk — they build things for you during conversation. A tracker, a decision framework, a pattern log. The artifact IS the coach's work product, created inline, living in conversation history.

CHRIS: 'I think my struggle is to help me try to figure out what my tension is right now. I think the struggle is I'm not sure what design to build or what designs to take further.' This was the moment we stopped asking UI questions and started addressing the real problem: not which prototype to pick, but how the coaching entry point grows into the workspace functionality from Rounds 1-12 without feeling like two different products.

CHRIS: 'The connection from some of these designs to the workspace concept feels disjoint. I'm trying to figure out how we start at coaching and then eventually allow all the functionality that we have captured in those other things, namely Round 11 v3/v4. The key is that we want to capture the functionality but I don't care about matching the layout or how we were going to do it.' This reframed the entire problem: the FUNCTIONALITY matters, the STRUCTURE doesn't. Coaching is how you enter. The workspace mechanics serve the coaching relationship, not the other way around.

THE BREAKTHROUGH QUOTE — Chris: 'Maybe the artifacts and all these other things aren't as important if you can render graphics and things inline in the chat and have them expand out in the side when you need them. It's like conversation first but then these tiling and all these other things that we see on the side are possible. You can play with those as necessary as you talk.' This is the design direction: conversation IS the product. Artifacts render inline. Expansion into side panels IS the workspace. No mode switch. No separate items panel. The workspace materializes from conversation content.

PROGRESSIVE REVEAL CONCEPT — Proposed three layers: check-in (the door) → coach circle (the room) → workspace (the desk). The founder doesn't start at the desk. They walk through the door. They sit in the room. They go to the desk when they need to. The memo confirms this: 'Each layer requires the trust earned by the previous one.' The workspace is the destination. Coaching is the path. We designed the destination first across 12 rounds. Now we designed the path.

UNIVERSAL PRIMITIVE — The agent-with-memory, not coaching itself. Maya the Strategic Coach and Chef the culinary collaborator are architecturally identical: a character with domain expertise, access to the memory graph, and a relationship that compounds. The UI doesn't change when you expand beyond coaching — you add areas and agents. The naming just can't hardcode 'coaching.' Coaching agents introduce themselves as coaches through personality, not UI chrome.

PERSONA RESULTS SUMMARY — 6 founders evaluated 10 wireframes.

Conversation+Drawer (#8): Won 5/6. 'Disarmingly simple' (Priya). 'Like David's office with the door shut at 11pm' (Elena). 'The only one that would get me to say the real thing' (Sarah). 'Not the flashiest. Probably the smartest' (Kenji).

Two-Column Journal (#9): Won 3/6 for longitudinal value. Elena almost cried. Sarah felt like there was a future version of herself. Only works if journal auto-generates from conversations.

Notebook (#4): Surprise winner for David (non-technical, chaos) — 'the containers loosened something in my chest.' Marcus was haunted by the empty Relationships folder.

Command Bar (#5): Official pick for power users, failed them at 1am. Priya: 'at 1am I don't have words for what I'm feeling, let alone a search query.'

Lobby (#10): Most screenshot-worthy, 0 real users. Kenji: 'design award energy, 3% retention.'

Coach-First Nav (#3): Consistently rejected — 'too intimate too early, feels like choosing a therapist.'

SYNTHESIS DESIGN — Conversation+Drawer+auto-Journal+coach-created-structure+expandable-artifacts. Validated by personas, refined through Agentation feedback. The product is: (1) Feed home with greeting, composer, proactive nudge, journal timeline. (2) Full-screen conversation with inline artifacts that expand to side panels. (3) Drawer holds Spaces, Journal, Your Stuff. (4) Coaches are the navigation — click to DM, 3-dot for profile. (5) Structure emerges from coaching — coaches create containers over time, not upfront categories. (6) Voice+text always available. (7) Mobile: bottom sheet drawer, full-screen artifact overlays. Desktop: inline sidebar with expand/collapse.

CHRIS ON VOICE: 'I do like voice as a core option but both voice and text I think should always be available because sometimes people won't be able to talk aloud.' Voice mode exists as a full-screen dark takeover (Luna coaching about sleep, waveform visualization, transcript). But every conversation surface has both a mic button and text input simultaneously. Not a mode toggle — both always present.

CHRIS ON MULTI-AGENT: 'Multi-agent is really important. I think you're asking the wrong questions.' Multi-coach in one conversation is not a nice-to-have — it's core. Maya helping with fundraise while Luna chimes in about sleep affecting pitch confidence. Rex referencing Luna's data. The cross-domain moment is the thing that's impossible with human coaches and impossible with ChatGPT. It needs to happen naturally in one conversation, not require switching spaces.

WHAT THE PROTOTYPE COVERS VS MEMO — All Layer 1 (coaching) features implemented: multi-coach, memory transparency, proactive nudges, accountability tracking, group coaching with trust boundaries, coach-created artifacts. Layer 2 (personalized software) partially shown — coaches create inline trackers/frameworks but no 'generated tools' yet. Open questions: artifact storage model, journal materialization, nudge triggers, voice API integration, trust boundary implementation, coach creation backend.

The answer emerged through iteration: (1) 'Your Stuff' tab in the drawer shows all coach-created artifacts, (2) Cmd+K search finds across conversations/journal/memory/artifacts, (3) coach profiles show artifacts they've created, (4) the auto-generated journal timeline surfaces key moments. Multiple paths to the same content, none requiring browsing a folder structure.

CHRIS ON TREATING COACHES LIKE PEOPLE: 'I think treating them like people is probably the closest. Going to my area or going to one coach or asking multiple coaches to help with some work making sense.' This informed the sidebar design: coaches are listed as a roster of people, not features or tools. You click a person to talk to them. The DM is implicit. STRUCTURE SHOULD EMERGE FROM COACHING — Don't make users categorize problems upfront. Let coaches create containers. After 5 conversations, Maya says 'I notice we keep coming back to three things — your fundraise, your co-founder tension, and your sleep. Want me to organize these?' ROUNDS 5-8: WORKSPACE EXPLORATION — Chris was still thinking of the UI as a workspace. The realization came from thinking about flow state — not seeing everything at once, but having exactly the information you need in front of you. A main chat with inline interactive content, plus one or two major tiles, gives you everything without the cognitive overload of a full workspace. 'It lets you just focus on what matters rather than trying to look at a bajillion things at once.' This was the seed of conversation-first thinking, even before the coaching pivot.

Chris: 'I basically just had this big realization that with the direction we're going we need to make this more front and center. The memo that I shared with you at the very start of this conversation sort of ties back into that.'

THE FULL ARC — 15 rounds telling one story:

Rounds 1-4: EXPLORE — 9 layout directions, killed 5, found the tension (simple ≠ capable). Popover browser and desktop tiling emerged as resolvers.

Rounds 5-6: CONVERGE ON TILING — Tiling + anchored chat became the pattern. Saved layouts per topic. Workspace energy.

Round 7: FLOW STATE REALIZATION — Chris realized workspace emphasis was wrong. Flow state isn't seeing everything — it's having exactly what you need. Chat + 1-2 tiles > full workspace.

Round 8: TWO FINALISTS — Arc-tiles (sidebar + chat as tile) and conversation-artifact (chat-first + artifact panel). Both validated, different trade-offs.

Rounds 9-10: VISUAL LANGUAGE — Applied Swiss editorial / Bauhaus-influenced brand language. 8 variants exploring color/weight/density. Consolidated into unified direction.

Round 11 v1-v3: REFINEMENT — Polishing the converged direction.

DESIGN DECISIONS from annotation review session:

THREADS:
- Any message can spawn a thread (hover action like Slack)
- Threads open in side panel (desktop) or push view (mobile)
- Thread list accessible both per-space (dropdown under space name in sidebar) AND globally (icon in top bar)
- Calls create a thread in the space with full transcript
- Threads cover the 'quick one-off question' use case that current AI tools solve

VOICE MODE:
- One active coach face that swaps when speaker changes (crossfade transition)
- Not a grid of faces — keeps intimacy
- Small faces at top show who else is in the call
- Coach icon pulses/glows to indicate speaking (no separate waveform)
- Future: PiP minimize into floating pill while main UI stays usable
- Call transcript streams into space as a thread

ARTIFACTS:
- Artifacts belong to spaces implicitly (created in conversation within a space)
- Three access paths: (1) sidebar — expand space to see threads + artifacts as children, (2) Your Stuff drawer tab — all artifacts grouped by space, (3) top bar icon — quick filter for current space's artifacts
- Sidebar stays clean at top level — depth reveals on expand
- Artifacts and threads are siblings under a space, differentiated by icon

CONVERSATION INFO:
- Participants clickable for coach profiles
- 'Manage participants' replaces 'Add a coach' — entry point for both humans and coaches

DMs vs Spaces: DMs are implicit 1:1 conversations tied to coach rows. Spaces are topic-scoped, long-lived, can have multiple coaches. DMs don't appear in Spaces list. Click coach → opens persistent DM (finds existing first). 3-dot menu → View profile / Edit / Remove.

Sharing: 'Manage participants' in convo info. For now coach-only. Humans added via Share flow (email invite + copy link). Trust note: shared humans see conversation but not private memories. Full team/workspace concept deferred.

Empty state: New DMs and spaces always start with a coach greeting. DM: 'Hey Alex. I'm here whenever you need to think something through. What's going on?' Named space: '[Topic] — let's get into it.' Coach greets with warm expression. No empty rooms.

Agentation annotation workflow is effective for rapid iteration. Chris leaves visual feedback on specific elements, we discuss uncertain items, implement clear ones immediately with commits, and capture design decisions for uncertain ones. The feel → react → fork loop works.

Threads and files are SPACE-SCOPED, not global. When you click the thread or file icon in the top bar, you see items from the current space only. This matches mental model: you're working in a space, you want that space's stuff. Global search (Cmd+K) handles cross-space discovery.

POPUP PATTERN: Both threads and files use the same ItemsPopup component — dropdown from top bar, space-scoped, flat chronological list, expandable to full modal with search. Consistent, predictable access pattern. Same component, different data.

SHARE UI: Conversation info now has a Share section with email invite + copy link. Trust note: 'They'll see the conversation and coaches, but not your private memories.' Human teammates are manual share for now — full workspace/team concept deferred to later.

COACHES → CONTACTS: Renamed 'Your Coaches' section to 'Contacts' throughout the UI. This opens the door for human contacts in the same list later. 'Create a contact' replaces 'Create a coach'. The section holds both AI coaches and (future) human teammates.

PHONE NUMBERS PER COACH: Each coach has a unique phone number for SMS. Users can text their coach from their regular messaging app without opening Pebble. Phone shown in profile (contact card with copy) and DM top bar (inline, tap to copy). Future: voice calls via same number.

COACH PROFILE AS MODAL: Coach profiles open as centered modals (520px desktop, 95% mobile) instead of full-screen overlays. All sections edit inline — click pencil, content transforms to editable state, click done. No screen navigation for edits.

COACH PROFILE SECTIONS: Four editable sections define a coach's identity: Coaching Style (paragraph), Specialties (tag pills), Personality (4 sliders: direct↔gentle, challenging↔supportive, structured↔freeform, proactive↔reactive), Vibe (short quote about their energy). Plus Appearance (shape + color picker).

V1 SIDEBAR: Always expanded, no collapse/minimize. Single 'Spaces' label (no tab bar). Spaces section with 3-dot menus (Archive), Contacts section with 3-dot menus (View Profile, Remove). 3-dot always visible on mobile, hover-only on desktop.

DM TOP BAR PATTERN: For DM conversations, the top bar mirrors the sidebar contact row — coach face (28px) + name on first line + role · phone on second line. For spaces/groups, single line with name + coach faces. Consistent visual language across sidebar and main content.

TWO PROTOTYPE STRATEGY: conversation-polished (full vision, 69 files) and conversation-minimal/Pebble Coaching V1 (build target, 45 files) coexist as separate families. Changes can be synced manually. The minimal version strips threads, voice, search, journal, settings, onboarding — just sidebar + chat + artifacts + creator flows.

Round 16 prototypes not clicking. Likely reasons: (1) they're reskins of the multi-coach design with one face instead of five — the layout didn't fundamentally change, (2) the hat/mode concept adds complexity to what should be a simplification, (3) the pet rock idea is cute but might feel gimmicky for a serious product, (4) none of them explore what a single deep AI relationship actually FEELS like — they still think in terms of spaces/modes/configurations rather than one unified companion.

SINGLE AGENT CHANGES THE PRODUCT FUNDAMENTALLY. With multiple coaches, memory was distributed — Maya knew fundraise, Luna knew sleep. With one agent, ALL memory is unified. Cross-domain awareness (sleep affects pitch confidence) is no longer a feature to surface — it's native to how the agent thinks. The product isn't a coaching platform with specialists. It's a personal AI companion. The vibe shifts from 'professional service' to 'deeply personal relationship.' This is a bigger shift than just replacing 5 faces with 1.

THE REAL QUESTION: what does it feel like to have ONE AI that knows everything about you? Not 'how do we organize spaces.' Not 'what hat does it wear.' The question is: what is the experience of a single entity that remembers your fundraise anxiety, your sleep patterns, your partner tension, your fitness goals — all as one connected picture of your life? The answer to that question IS the product. Everything else is organizational scaffolding.

7-PERSONA RESEARCH ON SPACE CREATION — Key convergences across all personas:

1. TALK FIRST, ORGANIZE LATER. Every persona (minimalist, power user, first-timer, switcher, non-technical, organizer, conversationalist) said the same thing: don't make me configure before I get value. Start with conversation, let structure emerge.

2. THE WORD 'SPACE' IS WRONG. David (non-technical): 'I'd call it a topic. Or a thing.' Everyone struggled with the word. 'Topic' or 'conversation' tested better than 'space', 'room', or 'channel.'

3. NO SETTINGS FOR SPACES. Universal agreement: no sliders, no forms, no configuration screens. The agent should infer behavior from the purpose/topic. Customization happens through conversation ('be tougher on me') not through UI.

4. SPACES SHOULD EMERGE FROM CONVERSATION. Kenji's 'emergent spaces' concept and Jordan's 'conversation becomes space' direction both propose the same thing: you talk, the agent notices patterns, suggests creating a space. You approve. The space inherits context. Best of both worlds: Priya (first-timer) said spaces should be introduced AFTER the first useful conversation, not before.

5. ONE QUESTION IS ENOUGH. For explicit creation: 'What do you want help with?' One text field. Name auto-derived. Icon auto-assigned or quick-pick. Done. Power user Marcus: 'One question forces me to say the thing that actually matters.'

6. GUARD AGAINST OVER-ORGANIZATION. Sarah (organizer): hard cap around 7 active spaces, auto-archive after 30 days idle, no tags, no colors (auto-assign), no descriptions. The product should make talking easier than organizing.

7. CROSS-SPACE MEMORY IS THE KILLER FEATURE. Elena (ChatGPT switcher): 'ChatGPT conversations are islands. If Pebble connects sleep to pitch confidence to relationship strain automatically, that alone justifies switching.' This is what one agent with unified memory enables that multi-agent couldn't.

8. THE FACE SHOULD NEVER CHANGE. One agent, one face, everywhere. David: 'I just want to talk to the same friend about different things.' The consistency IS the trust signal.

THE SINGLE-AGENT PIVOT — Chris and co-founder realized multiple agents 'doesn't make as much sense as we initially thought. Instead we want a single agent that wears different hats depending on the space it is in.' This is a fundamental product shift: from a coaching team (Maya, James, Luna, Rex, Aria) to one companion that adapts. The main entry is your main chat with your assistant, plus focused spaces for specific things.

HATS AS BEHAVIOR MODIFIERS — Chris: 'The goal is by giving them a hat it focuses on different domains. A fitness headband means it's more motivational, more structured, more scientific. A meditation coach would be more zen, more peaceful.' The hat isn't decoration — it changes HOW the agent shows up. But Chris pushed back on presets: 'People should be able to create them how they want. I think we don't know what all the cases are going to be so they need to be flexible.'

THE NAMING CRISIS — The concept doesn't fit existing metaphors. Chris: 'I don't want to be too tied to the idea of space or persona but in essence those are what I'm trying to solve for.' Spaces sound like Slack channels. Personas sound like configuration. Apps sound technical. Projects sound productivity-only. Conversations sound disposable. The thing being built is genuinely novel — a persistent environment where your AI companion helps with one specific part of your life using whatever tools and conversation style that area needs.

TWO-DOOR PROBLEM RESOLVED — In Pebble Coaching V1, sidebar had Spaces AND Contacts. User had to decide: go to a space or go to a coach? Same problem would recur with Spaces AND Personas. Chris: 'There shouldn't be options for both personas and space. Personas are a part of a space.' Resolution: a space IS topic + persona bundled. One list in the sidebar. One entry point. No meta-decision about how to start.

SPACE AS PURPOSE-BUILT ENVIRONMENT — Chris: 'In some ways it could be almost like an individual app where it's focused on helping you with one thing.' The fitness space isn't just conversations about fitness — it has trackers, progress charts, training plans. The Sequoia space has talking points, timeline, deck feedback. A space is closer to a mini-app that the agent builds and operates for a specific purpose. The conversation is the interface, but the space has structure beyond chat.

USE CASE SPECTRUM — Spaces range from mostly-conversation (therapy, venting, reflection) to mostly-tools (fitness tracking, budget management, learning). The UI tension: does it adapt per space or is there one universal layout? Chris leans toward chat + tiling (from earlier work) but isn't sure. Key question: conversation is universal (every space has chat) but tools are dynamic (agent creates what's needed per space).

LIKES: single stream concept (but needs navigation back), command bar (useful but unclear how), dashboard data (not as main page but interesting), some gamification (for stickiness, not everything), starter spaces (not forced but helpful), longer onboarding (can convert better), feed (useful, just not social media style).

DISLIKES: wireframe design direction (prefers warm colorful V1 style), swipeable cards, accordion, bubble contacts, pebble home, journal navigation, tagged stream (too techy), floating composer (doesn't feel like talking to a friend), stories (interesting but doesn't love it).

NUANCED: dashboard 'is interesting in a lot of ways', enterprise 'does a lot of things we think about', gamification 'there's a lot we can learn from', overcrowded 'does a lot of interesting stuff', bad ChatGPT 'only works if framed as long-term chat partner where conversations remember forever.'

EMERGING DIRECTION — Chris: 'You have your main conversation with the agent. As you do things and things come up, it creates this persistent environment. Those create a thread in the main conversation that can be pulled back up at any point as an interactive thing that opens up this thing they're working on. All the conversation in the main chat gets tied to that somehow.'

This means: main chat is the home. Persistent environments emerge from it. They can be opened alongside the main chat (tiling). Conversations in main chat connect to the relevant environment.

THE SINGLE-AGENT PIVOT — FOUNDER QUOTE — Chris: 'Instead we want a single agent that wears different hats depending on the space it is in.' This wasn't a minor adjustment — it invalidated the entire multi-coach architecture. The coaching beachhead had 6 named coaches (Maya, Luna, Kai, etc.) each with distinct personalities, shapes, and domains. The pivot collapsed all of them into one entity. The product went from 'team of specialists' to 'one partner who adapts.'

PET ROCK CONCEPT — The pebble-rock prototype introduced an organic stone SVG with face features (dot eyes, curved mouth, blush marks) and per-space hat accessories. A briefcase hat for work, a moon hat for sleep, a heart for relationships, a hardhat for building. The hat IS the behavior — not decoration. The pet rock becomes YOUR rock: nameable, customizable, a persistent companion that changes context through accessories rather than identity. This bridges the abstract 'single agent with hats' concept into something tangible and emotionally resonant.

CHRIS ON PRESETS — Pushed back hard on preset space configurations: 'People should be able to create them how they want.' The agent should adapt from a simple purpose statement, not from users selecting from predefined templates. Customization happens through conversation, not settings panels. One field — 'what do you want help with?' — and the agent builds the right environment from there.

HATS AS BEHAVIOR, NOT DECORATION — Chris: 'A fitness headband means it's more motivational, more structured.' Hats are behavior modifiers that change how the agent communicates: tone, focus areas, proactivity level, tool selection. The fitness hat doesn't just add a cute icon — it makes the agent track workouts, send accountability nudges, use motivational language. The fundraise hat makes it structured, analytical, document-focused. Same core personality, different operating mode.

SPACE CREATION FLOW DEBATE — The question: how much configuration before first conversation? Two poles emerged. One extreme: full setup wizard with purpose, behavior sliders, tool selection. Other extreme: one field ('what do you want help with?') and the agent figures it out. Chris strongly favored the minimal side — agent adapts from purpose, customization through conversation not settings. 'Hey Pebble, I want to get better at fitness' → agent creates the space, picks the right tools, sets the right tone. No configuration screens.

7-PERSONA RESEARCH — CONVERGENCE DETAILS. 7 personas tested (minimalist, power user, first-timer, ChatGPT switcher, non-technical, organizer, conversationalist). Key findings beyond the headline convergences:

1. The word 'space' doesn't resonate with non-technical users — sounds like Slack, feels corporate
2. Spaces should emerge from conversation, never be pre-created or configured upfront
3. Cross-space memory is THE killer feature vs ChatGPT — 'my fitness coach knowing I'm stressed from fundraising'
4. One face, never changes — even the most customization-hungry personas (power user, organizer) didn't want multiple agent faces
5. Even the ChatGPT switcher persona — someone actively comparing products — valued the relationship continuity over feature parity
